summary: Juridical sentencing record from Huari (Wari capital, Ayacucho) — a concealment case where a father (tata) hid assets and was sentenced. 91 cords, ALL L1 (flat, no subsidiaries), 100% STRING hit rate (highest in corpus). First word paka (secret) = thesis, last word tatata (father-ACC) = verdict. The father's grammatical trajectory through the khipu tells the story: plain -> passive -> peak fine (50) -> concealment attributed -> accusative (sentenced). Single B-colored cord at position 10 = the CHARGE (taka, to strike) highlighted by the ONLY color change. S-values track escalating fines totaling 580. Date: Mode B checkbox month 2. 64 STRING, 100% coverage.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  91 cords, ALL level 1 — no subsidiaries, flat structure.
  Architecture: 0-SEUL, single cluster (no section breaks).
  Color: AB (light brown) = 90/91 (one B cord at position 10).
  S-values present (total sum = 580). Directions: ALL SS.
  Provenance: Huari (Wari Empire capital, Ayacucho).
  Museum: Universidad de San Martin de Porres, Lima.
  Date: Mode B checkbox month 2.
  64 STRING (100% hits — every STRING cord confirmed), 27 INT, 0 EMPTY.

COLOR-AS-ROLE
  AB (light brown, 90 cords): The ENTIRE legal narrative — parties, acts, evidence, verdict.
  B (brown, 1 cord at position 10): THE CHARGE — a single taka (to strike), highlighted by the ONLY color change. The khipu equivalent of underlining one word.

FIRST WORD = THESIS
  Cord 1: paka (20) = 'The secret.' Before the father is named, before anyone is struck — the first word is THE SECRET. This case is about something hidden.

LAST WORD = VERDICT
  Cord 91: tatata = tata + ta = 'the father [as OBJECT of judgment].' The father is what the justice system acts UPON. He is sentenced.

THE GRAMMATICAL TRAJECTORY
  The father's grammatical journey tells the entire story:
    Cords 2-3: tata (plain) — 'The father' — he is named
    Cord 25: tata (20) (plain + fine) — a number is assigned
    Cord 45: tataka (PASSIVE) — 'The father was [affected]'
    Cord 54: tataka (PASSIVE) — repeated for emphasis
    Cord 58: tataka (30) (PASSIVE + fine) — fined 30
    Cord 84: tataka (PASSIVE) — again
    Cord 86: tata (50) (plain + PEAK fine) — the maximum
    Cord 87: katapa (GEN) — 'Of the concealment' — the crime attributed
    Cords 88-89: tataka x2 (20) — 'The father [judged], [fined 20]'
    Cord 91: tatata (ACCUSATIVE) — THE VERDICT
  From plain (named) -> passive (affected) -> peak fine (50) -> concealment attributed -> accusative (sentenced). The grammar IS the story.

KEY VOCABULARY
  taka (to strike x14, S-values 0-30) = assault, the charge (highlighted at cord 10 in B color)
  kaka (maternal uncle x10, S-values 0-30) = the contesting party
  tata (father x9, S-values 0-50) = the accused/sentenced party
  tataka (father-PASSIVE x6) = 'the father suffered/was judged'
  kama (to govern x3) = the presiding authority
  tama (herd x3) = disputed property
  tapa (guardianship x3) = the legal outcome
  paka (hidden/secret x2) = the thesis — first AND penultimate words
  pata (land x2) = disputed property
  kata (to conceal x1) = the formal charge
  kakaka (uncle-PASSIVE x2), kakama (toward uncle x1)
  katapa (of the concealment x1) = the crime named in possessive
  tatata (father-ACCUSATIVE x1) = THE VERDICT

S-VALUES AS FINES (total = 580)
  Opening: paka(20) + tata(10) = 30 — initial deposit
  Escalation: tata(20) + kakama(30) + taka(20) + tapa(20) + kama(30) + kaka(20) = 140
  Peak: tataka(30) + tata(30) + pata(30) + taka(30) + takaka(20) + pataka(20) + tapaka(30) = 190
  Verdict: tata(50) + tataka(20) + tapa(30) = 100

NARRATIVE — THE SECRET OF THE FATHER
  A secret was kept. That is the first word — paka, hidden, at cord 1. Something was concealed, and this document exists because it came to light.
  The father is named next. Tata, tata — cord 2, cord 3. Twice, as if making sure there is no mistake. Then the uncle: kaka, kaka — cord 4, cord 5. Also twice. The two parties, named in pairs.
  The governor: kama (cord 6). The authority who will judge. And immediately: taka (cord 7) — to strike. Violence enters four cords after it began.
  Herds are counted: tama x3. The property at stake.
  At cord 10, the scribe does something he does nowhere else. He changes color. Light brown gives way to plain brown. The word on this singular cord: taka — to strike. The charge, underlined.
  The numbers rise. Cord 21 carries 224 — by far the largest. Then kakama — toward the uncle's authority. Then kakaka — the uncle in the passive voice.
  The father appears six times in the passive voice. Six times the khipu says: the father SUFFERED.
  The concealment appears at cord 59: kata — to conceal. And cord 87: katapa — 'of the concealment.' The father's secret (paka, cord 1) is now a formal charge (kata, cord 59). What was hidden was THE FATHER'S crime. He hid something.
  The verdict: cord 86 carries tata with S-value 50 — the maximum. Then katapa (of the concealment), tataka x2 (the father judged), tapa (guardianship assigned, 30 units). And the last cord: tatata — the father, accusative. The object of justice. From paka to tatata, the secret to the sentenced father.

CROSS-CORPUS — THE JURIDICAL QUARTET
  UR039 vs UR110 vs UR112 vs LL01:
    UR039: First word paka (secret), last word tatata (father-ACC), crime=concealment, 100% hits
    UR110: crime=violence+concealment, S-values ALL ZERO
    UR112: crime=concealment (blind replication), 100% hits
    LL01: First word PASS-chaqa (slaughtered), crime=murder, 10 colors
  UR039 and UR112 are the purest juridical khipus (100% hits).
references: Sivan 2026, ALBA Project — Juridical sentencing analysis, grammatical trajectory, S-values as fines
Dating paper: Sivan 2026, 'Dating the Inca Archive' — UR039 checkbox month 2
Cross-corpus: UR110 (complete dossier, 1452), UR112 (concealment, blind replication), LL01 (murder, 1519)
Cerrón-Palomino 2003, Lingüística quechua — grammatical case system
